title: How the body creates nausea as a boundary communication

htmlContent:
The knot that speaks

You know that feeling. The one that rises up from somewhere deep. A tightening in your throat. A churning in your stomach. Suddenly the thought of food makes you want to run. Or the thought of that person. Or that place. Or that conversation you keep avoiding.

Nausea is not random. It's not a glitch in your biology. It's a message. A loud, undeniable signal from your nervous system that something is wrong. Something is not safe. Something needs to change.

I used to think my nausea was just anxiety. Just a physical reaction to stress that I needed to push through. I'd take deep breaths. I'd tell myself to calm down. I'd force myself to stay in situations that made me want to vomit because I thought that was strength. It wasn't. It was me ignoring my own boundaries.

Here's the thing. Your body doesn't lie. Your mind can rationalize anything. It can convince you that you're fine, that you're overreacting, that you just need to try harder. But your stomach? It knows the truth. And it will keep sending that signal until you listen.

The science of the gut-brain connection

Let's talk about what's actually happening when nausea shows up. It's not just in your head. It's in your entire body. Your gut is lined with millions of neurons. Scientists call it the enteric nervous system. It's basically a second brain, and it's in constant communication with the one in your skull.

Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote The Body Keeps the Score, spent decades showing us that trauma lives in the body. Not just in memories. Not just in thoughts. But in physical sensations that we can't talk our way out of. Nausea is one of those sensations. It's your body saying "no" when your mouth can't find the words.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, would say that nausea is a sign your vagus nerve is doing its job. The vagus nerve runs from your brainstem all the way down to your gut. It's the main highway of your parasympathetic nervous system. When it detects danger, it can shut down your digestive system. That shutdown feels like nausea. It's your body's way of saying "stop eating, stop processing, stop being open to input. Something is not right."

Right? Think about it. When have you felt most nauseous in your life? Probably not when everything was fine. Probably when something was deeply off. A relationship that was toxic. A job that was draining you. A situation where you felt trapped and couldn't find a way out.

Your body was trying to create a boundary for you. It was making the experience of staying so uncomfortable that you would have no choice but to leave.

When nausea becomes a chronic boundary

Here's where it gets complicated. For many of us, especially those with complex trauma, nausea isn't a one-time signal. It's a constant companion. You wake up feeling sick. You eat and feel worse. You avoid social situations because you're afraid you'll throw up. You've been to every doctor. They've run every test. Nothing is physically wrong. But the nausea persists.

That's because your nervous system has learned that boundaries are dangerous. Maybe you grew up in a house where saying "no" got you punished. Maybe you learned early that your needs didn't matter. That you had to be agreeable. That you had to take care of everyone else. Your mind forgot those lessons. But your body remembers.

Now, every time you need to set a boundary, your body does it for you. But it does it in a way that hurts. It makes you sick instead of letting you speak. It turns your stomach into a battleground where the war is about whether you're allowed to have a self.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium supports the vagus nerve and can help regulate that gut-brain connection. It's not a cure. Nothing is a cure. But it can take the edge off while you do the deeper work.

Stay with me here. I'm not saying your nausea is all in your head. I'm saying it's in your body, and your body is trying to protect you. The question is: what is it protecting you from? What boundary have you been unable to set? What situation have you been unable to leave? What person have you been unable to say no to?

These are hard questions. They might make you feel more nauseous. That's okay. That's the signal getting louder.

Learning to listen without reacting

The first step isn't to make the nausea go away. The first step is to listen to it. To sit with it. To ask it what it's trying to tell you.

I know that sounds weird. I know you want relief. I've been there. I've spent years trying to make my body stop screaming at me. But the screaming doesn't stop until you hear the message. Until you acknowledge the boundary that needs to be set.

Here's a practice that helps me. When the nausea comes, I don't fight it. I don't reach for the ginger tea or the antacid immediately. I pause. I put my hand on my stomach. I take a slow breath. And I say to myself: "My body is trying to tell me something. What is it?"

Sometimes the answer comes right away. "I don't want to go to this meeting." "I don't want to see this person." "I need to leave this situation." Other times it's murkier. A general sense of overwhelm. A feeling of being trapped. A memory I haven't fully processed.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) by Deb Dana. She breaks down how to map your nervous system and understand these signals. It's not about fixing yourself. It's about learning your own language.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did your stomach tighten? Did you feel a wave of something? That's information. That's your body speaking. Don't judge it. Don't try to change it. Just notice.

Setting the boundary your body already knows

Eventually, the work is about action. It's about taking the information your nausea is giving you and using it to set a real boundary. In the real world. With real consequences.

This is the hard part. Because setting boundaries often means disappointing people. It means risking rejection. It means feeling guilty. And for those of us with trauma histories, guilt can feel like death. But the alternative is chronic nausea. A body that never settles. A life lived in survival mode.

I had a client once who had nausea every single morning for three years. She'd been to every specialist. She'd tried every diet. Nothing worked. Then we started looking at her life. She was in a relationship she didn't want to be in. She was working a job she hated. She was taking care of her aging parents alone because her siblings "couldn't handle it." She had no boundaries. None.

When she finally started setting them, the nausea began to fade. Not overnight. Not perfectly. But it got quieter. Because her body knew she was finally listening. It didn't have to scream anymore.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link) by Gabor Mate. He shows how suppressed boundaries and repressed emotions lead to physical symptoms. It's a hard read. But it might change how you see your nausea forever.

A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). Sometimes nausea is connected to sensory overload. Reducing input can give your nervous system a chance to settle.

An open ending

I don't have a neat conclusion for you. I don't have a five-step plan to make your nausea disappear. Because the truth is, it might not disappear. It might always be there, in some form, as a signal. But you can learn to hear it differently. You can learn to act on it instead of fighting it.

Your nausea is not your enemy. It's your body's most honest voice. It's the part of you that still knows what you need, even when your mind has forgotten. Even when your trauma has taught you to ignore yourself.

So the next time that knot forms in your stomach, pause. Ask it what it wants. And if you can, give it what it's asking for. Even if it's just one small boundary. Even if it's just one "no." Your body will thank you.

It might even stop screaming.
